purpose: Explains the sign of stock-bond correlation as a function of the dominant macroeconomic shock type (demand vs. supply) mediated by the central bank reaction function
components: Demand shock identification (growth-driven → negative correlation)
Supply shock identification (inflation-driven → positive correlation)
Central bank reaction function (Taylor Rule coefficient φ_π)
Phillips Curve slope as mediating parameter
Inflation expectations anchoring as boundary condition
current_assessment: All eight agents anchor on this framework. Current ambiguous shock mix (residual supply-side inflation + demand softening) places the US in the framework's indeterminate zone. The effective Taylor coefficient has diverged from textbook values (1.0-1.2 vs. 1.5-2.0), and fiscal dominance may make the reaction function endogenous — a modification but not a falsification of the framework.
limitations: Assumes exogenous central bank reaction function; fiscal dominance makes it endogenous
Phillips Curve slope (0.1-0.5) is a critical parameter the literature cannot pin down
Mixed-shock environments produce indeterminate predictions
Does not incorporate fiscal dynamics as an independent channel
Maturity-dependent correlation bifurcation requires extending the framework

purpose: Provides operationally actionable early-warning system for correlation regime flips with ranked leading indicators, lead times, and approximate hit rates
components: Tier 1: IG credit spreads widening >75bp from trough (18-month lead, 6/7 hit rate)
Tier 2a: ISM manufacturing <47 for 2+ consecutive months (12-month lead, 5/6)
Tier 2b: Yield curve un-inversion (6-12 months, 4/5)
Tier 3: Initial claims >300K (3-6 months, 7/8)
Tier 4: Fed cumulative cuts >100bp (1-3 months, ~100%)
Current status (March 2026): Only Tier 2b active
current_assessment: Most operationally actionable output. Probability of correlation flip within 12 months is below the historical base rate for multi-tier confirmation. Next critical signals: IG OAS crossing 150bp or ISM sustaining below 47. Sequencing logic is grounded in transmission mechanism (credit → manufacturing → labor → Fed).
limitations: Small sample (7 transitions) limits statistical power
Hit rates should be treated as approximate, not precise probabilities
Current fiscal environment may change lead times
Hit rates may not be stable across different macro regimes

purpose: Maps the two-dimensional parameter space (inflation × fiscal deficit) that determines whether stock-bond correlation is structurally stable or unstable
components: Core inflation dimension: <2.0% → negative correlation stable; 2.0-3.5% → unstable; >4.0% → positive correlation locks in
Fiscal deficit dimension: <4.0% declining → negative correlation; 4.5-7.0% → unstable; potentially higher → positive locks in
Current US position: core PCE ~2.7%, deficit ~6.5% GDP (inside corridor)
Exit conditions: movement outside corridor on either dimension resolves regime
current_assessment: More operationally useful than unidimensional inflation focus. The US clearly occupies the corridor. Parameter boundaries carry ±1pp uncertainty. The concept is more robust than the specific numerical boundaries. Derived from 4 G10 episodes.
limitations: Parameter boundaries derive from small sample (4 G10 episodes)
±1pp boundary uncertainty is significant relative to corridor width
Does not specify dynamics within the corridor (path dependency)
May not account for structural breaks (AI productivity, passive investing)

purpose: Specifies falsifiable conditions that must be simultaneously met for positive stock-bond correlation to lock in persistently
components: Condition 1: Core inflation exceeds central bank tolerance threshold
Condition 2: Adjusted term premium exceeds 100bp
Condition 3: Output gap near zero (preventing demand-channel offset)
Dollar break below DXY 100 as supplementary FX condition (fx_strategist_01 extension)
Current status: Two of three conditions met or approaching
current_assessment: Falsifiable specification that fits all observed transitions. Two of three conditions are met or approaching. The conjunction requirement explains why positive correlation has not locked in despite some conditions being present. The FX extension adds explanatory power for why oscillation continues.
limitations: Derived from limited historical transitions
Specific thresholds may need recalibration for current fiscal environment
Does not specify which condition is binding
Adjusted term premium estimation itself carries significant uncertainty (50-130bp range)

purpose: Provides an explicit probability-weighted model of the correlation distribution for portfolio construction, showing why unconditional correlation estimates are misleading
components: Regime D (demand-dominant): 55-60% probability, correlation -0.30 to -0.50
Regime S (supply-dominant): 40-45% probability, correlation +0.20 to +0.40
Unconditional average: approximately -0.06 (near zero)
Critical insight: distribution is bimodal, invalidating mean-variance optimization with the unconditional estimate
current_assessment: More actionable for portfolio construction than conjunction triggers. Unconditional near-zero correlation is robust to ±0.15 perturbations in regime weights. Regime probabilities are calibrated judgment rather than model output. Portfolio optimization using the unconditional correlation would be suboptimal.
limitations: Regime probabilities are calibrated judgment, not derived from a formal model
Two-regime assumption may be oversimplified (transitional subregimes exist)
Does not specify transition dynamics between regimes
Weights may not be stable over the 2-3 year forecast horizon

purpose: Provides systematic assessment of which monetary policy transmission channels are functioning vs. broken, explaining why the correlation regime has not resolved despite shock mix evolution
components: Interest rate channel: ~60% effective (mortgage lock-in effect)
Credit channel: ~80% effective (still working)
Asset price channel: negative effectiveness (AI rally counterproductive)
Exchange rate channel: ~50% effective (goods only 25% of core PCE)
Expectations channel: ~50-70% effective (forward guidance attenuated)
FCI miscalibration: 75-150bp in policy-rate-equivalent terms
current_assessment: Single most policy-relevant insight per pair_2. Explains the FCI loosening paradox (Goldman FCI eased ~1.0 point despite restrictive rates). The broken asset price channel is the critical finding — AI equity rally loosened conditions through a channel orthogonal to monetary policy. This explains why the regime transition has been slower than historical precedents.
limitations: Effectiveness percentages are estimated, not precisely measured
FCI miscalibration magnitude highly uncertain (40-200bp range)
Unclear whether transmission breakdown is cyclical or structural
Channel interactions may be nonlinear and are not captured

purpose: Identifies and categorizes historical episodes where fiscal-monetary conflict drove correlation transitions, providing duration estimates and structural comparisons
components: US 1966-71 fiscal stalemate (5 years)
UK 1972-77 fiscal stalemate (5 years)
Italy 2008-14 fiscal stalemate (6 years)
Implied duration for fiscal stalemate transitions: 5-7 years (vs. 12-36 months for monetary-only)
2001 recession as highest-similarity recession-contingent analogue
Nifty Fifty era as equity bifurcation analogue
current_assessment: The fiscal stalemate analogue class was judged more appropriate for the current environment than monetary-only transition analogues. Current transition is 4 years old, suggesting 1-3 more years. However, current fiscal starting point (6-7% deficit, ~125% debt/GDP) exceeds all three precedents, creating extrapolation risk.
limitations: n=3 for fiscal stalemate episodes is a thin analogue base
Varying institutional contexts across episodes limit comparability
Current fiscal position exceeds all precedents, possibly invalidating duration estimates
Italy analogue limited by currency union context and bank-heavy equity market
